title: Africanized Honeybee
gross_fact: When a hive feels threatened, hundreds can chase an intruder for a long distance, stinging in waves until the danger is gone.

title: Asian Giant Hornet
gross_fact: Its jaws can snip honeybees apart, and a small squad can wipe out a hive before carrying larvae home as food.

title: Assassin Bug
gross_fact: It stabs prey with a sharp beak, injects digestive fluid, then drinks the liquefied insides like a bug smoothie.

title: Australian Funnel Web Spider
gross_fact: Its fangs can punch through soft shoes, and its venom attacks nerves fast, making it one of Australia’s most feared spiders.

title: Aye-Aye
gross_fact: It taps trees with a long bony finger, hears hollow tunnels, then hooks out grubs like snacks from a wooden vending machine.

title: Bat-Eating Centipede
gross_fact: This giant centipede climbs cave ceilings, grabs bats from above, and hangs there while feeding on them.

title: Black Dragonfish
gross_fact: Females have needle teeth and their own red light, letting them glow-hunt in deep water where many prey cannot see red.

title: Black Swallower
gross_fact: It can gulp down fish bigger than itself, stretching its stomach like a living balloon in the dark sea.

title: Blue Dragon Sea Slug
gross_fact: It steals stinging cells from venomous jellyfish and stores them in its own body for defense.

title: Blue-Ringed Octopus
gross_fact: Tiny and beautiful, it flashes blue rings when alarmed and carries venom strong enough to paralyze prey in minutes.

title: Bombardier Beetle
gross_fact: When attacked, it blasts a boiling chemical spray from its rear with a popping sound.

title: Botfly Larva
gross_fact: Some botfly larvae grow under mammal skin, breathing through a tiny hole until they are ready to crawl out.

title: Brain Worm
gross_fact: The brain worm can wander through an animal’s nervous system, confusing movement and causing strange behavior.

title: Bristle Worm
gross_fact: Its body is covered in tiny brittle hairs that can snap into skin and sting like fiberglass splinters.

title: Bullet ant
gross_fact: Its sting is famous for pain so intense people compare it to being shot by a bullet.

title: Candiru Fish
gross_fact: This tiny Amazon catfish is surrounded by scary legends because it can follow chemical traces in water to find hosts.

title: Cassowary
gross_fact: This huge bird has a dagger-like claw on each foot and can kick hard enough to seriously injure a predator.

title: Cone Snail
gross_fact: It fires a tiny venom harpoon into prey, and some species carry venom powerful enough to stop a fish almost instantly.

title: Deathstalker Scorpion
gross_fact: Its sting packs a dangerous nerve toxin, and its glowing body looks extra creepy under ultraviolet light.

title: Deep Sea Lizardfish
gross_fact: It waits on the seafloor with huge jaws and needle teeth, snapping up prey in the black deep sea.

title: Electric Eel
gross_fact: It can release powerful electric shocks to stun fish, scare predators, and sense the muddy water around it.

title: Eyeless Spider
gross_fact: This cave spider lost its eyes over time, hunting in total darkness by touch and vibration.

title: Flesh Fly
gross_fact: Some flesh flies give birth to live maggots instead of laying eggs, so the larvae can start feeding immediately.

title: Flying Snake
gross_fact: It flattens its body into a ribbon shape and glides between trees, steering through the air without wings.

title: Gharial
gross_fact: Its long narrow jaws are lined with many sharp teeth, perfect for snapping fish from fast rivers.

title: Giant Isopod
gross_fact: This deep-sea cousin of a woodlouse can curl up like armor and survive long stretches with almost no food.

title: Goblin Shark
gross_fact: Its jaws shoot forward from its face to snatch prey, making it look like the shark has a spring-loaded mouth.

title: Goliath Birdeater
gross_fact: This huge tarantula can rub hairs from its body that irritate skin and eyes, then hide while attackers itch.

title: Goliath Tigerfish
gross_fact: Its giant teeth lock together like knives, and it can rip into fish in fast African rivers.

title: Guinea Worm
gross_fact: This parasite grows inside the body, then slowly emerges through the skin, sometimes over several painful days.

title: Hag Moth Caterpillar
gross_fact: Its fuzzy arms hide venomous spines that can cause a painful sting if touched.

title: Hagfish
gross_fact: When grabbed, it floods the attacker with slime that can clog gills and turn water into goo.

title: Hammerhead Worm
gross_fact: If cut into pieces, some hammerhead worms can regrow into more worms, making them creepy garden survivors.

title: Harlequin Toad
gross_fact: Many harlequin toads have skin toxins and bright warning colors that say: eating me is a terrible idea.

title: Hooded Pitohui
gross_fact: This bird carries poison in its skin and feathers, likely borrowed from toxic beetles it eats.

title: Horse Botfly
gross_fact: Its eggs hatch after being licked by a horse, and the larvae live inside the horse’s stomach before leaving in dung.

title: Horsehair Worm
gross_fact: This parasite grows inside insects, then can drive them toward water so the worm can escape and breed.

title: Hypnotic Stargazer Worm
gross_fact: It hides in sand with only its head exposed, waiting to grab small sea creatures that drift too close.

title: Immortal Jellyfish
gross_fact: When badly stressed, it can reverse into an earlier life stage, almost like hitting a jellyfish reset button.

title: Inland Taipan
gross_fact: Often called the most venomous snake, it carries venom adapted to quickly overpower small mammals.

title: Japanese Spider Crab
gross_fact: Its legs can span wider than a tall adult, making it look like a giant walking sea skeleton.

title: Kissing Bug
gross_fact: It feeds on blood near the face while animals sleep, and some carry a parasite that can cause Chagas disease.

title: Komodo Dragon
gross_fact: Its bite can tear flesh, and its saliva contains bacteria and venom-like compounds that help weaken prey.

title: Lamprey
gross_fact: It clamps onto fish with a round toothy mouth, rasping a hole and feeding on blood and body fluids.

title: Maggot of the Flesh Fly
gross_fact: These larvae can appear in decaying meat or wounds and start feeding fast because some flesh flies birth them alive.

title: Mouth Brooding Fish
gross_fact: Some parents protect eggs or babies inside their mouths, going without normal feeding while the young hide there.

title: Musk Ox Parasite Fly
gross_fact: Its larvae live inside musk oxen as internal parasites, feeding safely while the host carries them around.

title: Nose-Horned Viper
gross_fact: This viper has a small horn on its snout and venom used to subdue prey before swallowing it whole.

title: Parasitic Barnacle
gross_fact: Some parasitic barnacles invade crabs and can control their bodies, making them care for barnacle young.

title: Peacock Mantis Shrimp
gross_fact: It punches with a club so fast the water flashes with tiny shock bubbles that can crack shells.

title: Pistol Shrimp
gross_fact: It snaps one claw so fast it creates a shockwave bubble loud enough to stun prey.

title: Poison Dart Frog
gross_fact: Some species carry skin toxins so strong that predators learn to avoid their bright warning colors.

title: Rat-Tailed Maggot
gross_fact: It breathes through a long tube like a snorkel while wriggling in dirty, low-oxygen water.

title: Red Paper Wasp
gross_fact: It builds papery nests from chewed wood and can deliver repeated stings when the colony is disturbed.

title: Saddleback Caterpillar
gross_fact: Its cute green saddle hides venomous spines that can sting with sharp burning pain.

title: Scaly-Foot Snail
gross_fact: This deep-sea snail grows iron-rich scales on its foot, like a tiny armored knight near volcanic vents.

title: Sea Cucumber
gross_fact: When threatened, some sea cucumbers shoot sticky guts or toxic threads from their bodies to distract attackers.

title: Sea Devil Anglerfish
gross_fact: The tiny male can fuse to the huge female, sharing her bloodstream while she keeps his body attached.

title: Sea Spider
gross_fact: Some sea spiders are so thin their organs extend into their legs because there is little room in the body.

title: Spiny Oak Slug Caterpillar
gross_fact: Its bright spines can inject irritating venom, turning a curious touch into instant regret.

title: Stargazer Fish
gross_fact: It hides buried in sand with eyes on top, then lunges upward to grab prey passing overhead.

title: Stink Bug
gross_fact: When bothered, it releases a foul-smelling chemical cloud that makes predators spit it out.

title: STONEFISH
gross_fact: It looks like a rock, but its back spines can inject extremely painful venom if stepped on.

title: Surinam Toad
gross_fact: Babies grow in pockets on the mother’s back, then pop out as tiny toads from her skin.

title: Terrible Claw Lobster
gross_fact: Its long spiny claws look oversized for its body, built for grabbing prey in the deep sea.

title: Tiger Leech
gross_fact: It slices skin with tiny jaws and drinks blood while its saliva helps keep the wound flowing.

title: Tongue Worm
gross_fact: This parasite can live in the airways of reptiles and mammals, using hooks to hold on inside the host.

title: Tongue-Eating Louse
gross_fact: It attaches to a fish’s tongue, destroys it, then sits in its place and feeds on blood and mucus.

title: Tsetse Fly
gross_fact: Its bite can spread sleeping sickness parasites, which move through the blood and can affect the brain.

title: Vampire Bat
gross_fact: It makes a tiny cut, laps blood, and uses saliva chemicals that keep the wound from clotting quickly.

title: Vampire Fish (Payara)
gross_fact: Its lower fangs can grow so long they fit into special holes in the upper jaw when the mouth closes.

title: Velvet Worm
gross_fact: It fires sticky slime from nozzles on its head, trapping prey before biting into it.

title: Zombie Ant Fungus
gross_fact: This fungus takes over an ant’s behavior, makes it climb, bite down, and die where spores can spread.

title: Zombie Snail
gross_fact: A parasite makes the snail’s eyestalks pulse like caterpillars, luring birds that continue the parasite’s life cycle.

title: Zombie Starfish
gross_fact: Some starfish can lose an arm to danger, and the crawling piece may help distract predators while the body escapes.
